zotero.abstractNote: Contrastive learning has emerged as a powerful paradigm for self-supervised representation learning. Despite its empirical success, a theoretical understanding of why contrastive learning works so well remains elusive. In this paper, we show that contrastive learning can be understood as an inversion of the data generating process. Specifically, we prove that under a generative model of the data, the contrastive loss objective is equivalent to maximizing the mutual information between the representation and the latent factors of variation, provided that the encoder is sufficiently expressive. We further show that this objective leads to representations that are disentangled and invariant to nuisance factors. We validate our theoretical findings with extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets, demonstrating that contrastive learning indeed recovers the underlying latent factors of variation.
